retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: An exception to the previously stated UOP goal applies to patients with rhabdomyolysis and/or acute renal failure, where mortality can reach as high as 70% in severe burns. In these cases, fluids should be administered at a rate that achieves a UOP of 1 mL/kg/h. [30] However, notably, "more is not better," as the risk of fluid overload can be as life-threatening as the burn injury itself. Neither mannitol nor sodium bicarbonate is more effective in treating acute renal failure than fluid loading alone. [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Parkland Formula -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Lactated Ringer solution is the preferred choice of resuscitative crystalloid solution as it effectively treats both hypovolemia and extracellular sodium deficits caused by burn injury. The crystalloid is isotonic, closer in plasma composition than normal saline solution, inexpensive, readily available, and easily stored. Normal saline is typically avoided in large quantities as a resuscitative fluid option in burn patients, as administration can lead to hyperchloremic acidosis. [7] [8] [9] [10] All fluids administered before arrival should be accounted for and subtracted from the amount of fluid administered in the first 8 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Crystalloid fluids are the preferred choice for burn resuscitation due to their isotonic composition, which closely resembles plasma and helps achieve better outcomes. Hypotonic fluids are avoided as they can exacerbate edema. [10] Lactated Ringer solution is recommended for initial resuscitation across all age groups. However, infants are more prone to hypoglycemia due to limited glycogen stores, necessitating the addition of dextrose. Pediatric burn resuscitation formulas may include maintenance fluids with or without dextrose, depending on the child's age. [20] [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Resuscitation and Management(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- The Formula
retrieved_docs.content: Four mL lactated ringers solution × %TBSA burned × patient's weight in kilograms = total amount of fluid given in the first 24 hours. One-half of this fluid should be given in the first 8 hours. For example, a 75 kg patient with 55% total body surface area burn would need 4 mL LR × 75kg × 55% TBSA = 16,500 mL in the first 24 hours, with 8,250 mL in the first 8 hours or approximately 1 liter/hr for the first 8 hours. The Parkland Formula can be used for pediatric patients, plus normal maintenance fluids can be added. [16] [17] [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertonic Fluids -- Administration -- Hypertonic Saline
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with severe hyponatremia, serum sodium should undergo correction by 4 to 6 mEq/L per day, which can be achieved with 100 mL boluses of 3% HS at 10-minute intervals up to three total boluses. Some authorities recommend up to  8 mEq/L per day. [8] Less severe hyponatremia can achieve control with enough hypertonic saline to manage symptoms. [9] Due to the insufficient number of patients over age 65 in various trials, hypertonic fluids should start at the lowest ends of the dosing scale in the geriatric population. Pediatric traumatic brain injury generally receives treatment with a 6.5 to 10 mL/kg bolus of hypertonic saline. [10] Administration via a peripheral intravenous catheter is acceptable if no other access is available, but central venous access is the preferred route.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The formulas that use TBSA include the Parkland formula, which administers 4 mL/kg/%TBSA, and the modified Brooke formula, which uses 3 mL/kg/%TBSA. [11] The Cincinnati formula retains TBSA while also incorporating BSA, recommending 4 mL/kg/%TBSA of lactated Ringer solution plus 1500 mL/m² of total BSA. In contrast, the Galveston formula uses only BSA for fluid resuscitation, recommending 5000 mL/m² of BSA burned plus 2000 mL/m² of total BSA of lactated Ringer solution. Both the Cincinnati and Galveston formulas may include colloid in their resuscitation protocols. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Gluconate -- Administration -- Hydrofluoric Acid Burns
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium gluconate can be used as a 2.5% gel to treat hydrofluoric burns. This gel can be made with 25 mL of 10% calcium gluconate with 75 mL of glycerol (glycerine) and hydroxyethylcellulose gel. The gel is applied liberally and massaged into the burn area for 30 to 60 minutes, with reapplication as necessary. For persistent pain in burns, 5% calcium gluconate solution can be injected subcutaneously with a 27-gauge needle 0.5 cm away from the burning border into the surrounding unaffected area beneath and into the burn area (0.5mL/cm 2 of burn surface area).

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Various resuscitation formulas are used for burn management, with the Parkland and modified Brooke formulas being the most common in adults. [24] Developed in 1968 by Dr Charles Baxter, the Parkland formula is widely recognized for fluid replacement in burn injuries. The formula recommends administering 4 mL of lactated Ringer solution per kilogram of body weight per percentage of TBSA burned (4 mL/kg/%TBSA) in adults and 3 mL/kg/%TBSA in pediatric patients, with half given in the first 8 hours and the remainder over the next 16 hours. Discrepancies persist in patients where body surface area (BSA) calculations are unreliable, such as in pediatric patients and those with obesity. When first introduced, the Parkland formula was unique for recommending higher fluid volumes compared to its predecessors. The modified Brooke formula, similar to the Parkland formula, recommends 2 mL/kg/%TBSA for adults. [11] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Parkland Formula ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Resuscitation and Management(Archived) -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Excessive fluids are contraindicated in the hemodynamically stable burn patient, which likely contributes to edema.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initial TBSA calculation will guide the need for resuscitation and resuscitation fluid rates.  Burn resuscitation may be the most important and also one of the most controversial elements of burn care, with many differing opinions among experts. The 2012 American Burn Association Consensus acknowledges 20% TBSA as criteria for requiring formal resuscitation but also recognizes that pediatric burns may need different criteria, and many institutions have individualized guidelines for this patient population. They also acknowledge that there is insufficient evidence that more expensive colloid resuscitation is advantageous than crystalloid resuscitation. The most popular burn resuscitation formulas are the Parkland formula (4-times %TBSA x weight in kg) and the newer Modified Brooke formula (2-times %TBSA x weight in kg). They both calculate a number with units in milliliters. The first half of this calculated volume is divided evenly over the first 8 hours and the second half is divided evenly over the second 16 hours. Both original formulas advocated the use of colloid but more recently lactated ringers have been the resuscitation fluid of choice. After initial resuscitation rates are calculated, both formulas also advocate titration of rates to markers of physiologic response such as urine output of 0.5-1cc/kg/hr, and one should be aware that over-resuscitation can be as detrimental as under-resuscitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Parkland Formula -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Though adequate fluid resuscitation is essential during the initial 24 hours after injury, burn patients must remain adequately hydrated throughout their recovery. After 24 hours, the fluid should be switched from Lactated Ringer solution to 5% dextrose in half normal saline (D5½NS) and administered at a maintenance rate using the 4-2-1 rule as a guide. Recent practice guidelines for burn resuscitation consider the supplementation of colloid-containing fluid ranging from the first 8 hours to the first 24 hours post-burn, as it has been shown to decrease overall fluid requirements. [10] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients often arrive at burn centers after receiving excessive hydration during transport, a result of inexperienced first responders or clinicians who may overestimate the burn size. [33] [5] Additional factors have been identified that predispose burn patients to increased fluid requirements, including inhalation injury, delays in resuscitation, polytrauma, or high-voltage electrical injury. [34] The phenomenon referred to as "fluid creep" poses a challenge in burn management, as over-resuscitation can lead to detrimental outcomes, such as pulmonary and cerebral edema, or compartment syndrome of the extremities or abdomen. [11] [33] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Evaluation and Management(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For burns classified as severe (> 20% TBSA), fluid resuscitation should be initiated to maintain urine output > 0.5 mL/kg/hour.  One commonly used fluid resuscitation formula is the Parkland formula. The total amount of fluid to be given during the initial 24 hours = 4 ml of LR × patient’s weight (kg) × % TBSA.  Half the calculated amount is administered during the first eight hours, beginning when the patient is initially burned. For example, if a 70 kg patient has a 30% TBSA partial thickness burn, they will need 8400 mL Lactated Ringer solution in the first 24 hours with 4200 mL of that total in the first 8 hours [(4 mL) × (70 kg) × (30% TBSA) = 8,400 mL LR]. Remember that the fluid resuscitation formula for burns is only an estimate, and the patient may need more or less fluid based on vital signs, urine output, other injuries, or other medical conditions (see Burns, Resuscitation, and Management for discussion of the management of severely burned patients).

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid resuscitation is required for burns exceeding 20% total body surface area in adults and 10% to 15% in children. Lactated Ringer solution is the preferred crystalloid for initial resuscitation, while pediatric patients may require dextrose supplementation. Over-resuscitation, or "fluid creep," can cause complications such as pulmonary edema and compartment syndrome. Individualized fluid management remains essential to improving patient outcomes. This activity underscores the importance of fluid resuscitation in burn management and highlights the importance of interprofessional collaboration among healthcare providers in optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Burns -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Airway compromise and inhalation injury are common with severely burned patients or patients with significant facial burns. Supplemental oxygen should be given to any patient sustaining facial burns to treat any concomitant carbon monoxide or cyanide poisoning. [23] Severe burns to the face imply a prolonged contact with thermal insult, placing the upper airway at risk. Burns cause a local cytokine-mediated inflammatory response, creating hyperpermeability of the microvasculature, leading to tissue swelling. For the patient who sustains any facial burns or inhalation injuries, local swelling can occur rapidly and immediately. Physiochemical changes in the extracellular spaces cause protein denaturation, increasing the oncotic pressures, increasing local edema. It is also important to be aware of the requirement for fluid resuscitation, which increases the hydrostatic gradient, ultimately pushing more fluid into the extracellular space, compounding the tissue edema from the initial insult. All this results in edema accumulating over the first 6-8 hours, and continuing to develop over the next day. Advanced Burn Life Support (ABLS) criteria emphasize the importance of securing an airway early to prevent much more difficult intubation later. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Burns -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Burns involving 15 to 20% TBSA will likely develop hypovolemic shock, and aggressive early fluid resuscitation is indicated. Vascular access can be gained through burn tissue if needed, but avoiding burn tissue is preferred. Intraosseous access and central lines are acceptable alternatives. Burns with less than 10% TBSA rarely cause hemodynamic changes, and aggressive fluid rehydration is not indicated. It is recommended to start fluids initially during the primary phase and calculate the Parkland formula during the secondary survey. Ongoing resuscitation is titrated to physiologic response for long-term management. The goal is 0.5 mL/kg/h urinary output in the adult or 1 mL/kg/h in children (less than 14 years old or less than 25 kg). Urinary output exceeding these numbers increases the risk of worsening edema, causing worse clinical outcomes. Consider inserting a Foley catheter for further titration of fluids in regards to urinary output. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: clarifies how sodium‑based compounds, amino‑acid additives, antifibrinolytic agents, and irrigating solutions are interrelated and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous or irrigating formulations for fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specific type of IV solution used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. By creating an osmotic gradient that draws water into the renal tubules, these solutions promote diuresis, helping to manage fluid balance, correct electrolyte disturbances, and support nutritional needs in patients requiring intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 782
retrieved_docs.content: Blood and related products include a subcategory called blood substitutes and perfusion solutions. Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions, in turn, encompass IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. Within this group of IV solutions, there are specific formulations identified as solutions producing osmotic diuresis. Thus, solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specialized type of IV solution used for parenteral delivery of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, and these IV solutions are considered part of the broader class of blood substitutes and perfusion solutions that belong to the overall group of blood and related products. This hierarchical organization helps clinicians understand how various fluid therapies relate to one another and supports appropriate selection of products for volume replacement, electrolyte management, or targeted diuretic effect in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchy of intravenous and irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and related pharmacologic agents, emphasizing their therapeutic indications, contraindications, and connections to thromboembolic and shock states. CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is classified within Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance and is used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients; it is also grouped with antidiarrheals and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting combined electrolyte replacement and gastrointestinal therapeutic roles. BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is a high‑level category that includes irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and perfusion products, serving as the parent class for both “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” and “IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients,” and it subsumes the broader group blood and related products. Within this

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 900
retrieved_docs.content: it to other halide salts with similar ionic properties. Together, magnesium chloride and potassium chloride provide essential electrolyte homeostasis across oral, intravenous, intra‑ocular and cardiac therapeutic domains, supporting allergy management, fluid resuscitation, myocardial protection, and ocular irrigation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: cardioplegic electrolyte additives are integrated within a structured pharmacologic framework that supports fluid‑electrolyte management, organ protection, and therapeutic interventions in cardiac surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2548
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are intravenous solutions used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 629
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses both irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion and iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. Within the irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion group, the subclass Other irrigating solutions in ATC is defined, and this subclass is noted to contain the compound glycin. The iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients also include a specific subclass, Solutions producing osmotic diuresis, which are formulated to induce osmotic diuresis. Additionally, BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is considered part of the larger group of blood and related products, linking it to other blood‑derived therapeutics. These hierarchical relationships clarify how various fluid and perfusion products are organized within pharmacological classifications and how specific formulations, such as those containing glycin or designed for osmotic diuresis, fit into the overall therapeutic landscape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: preparations are linked to the broader group “Electrolytes with Carbohydrates, Antidiarrheals, Intestinal Anti‑inflammatory/Anti‑infective Agents” and are members of “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance.” These solutions belong to the larger class of intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, a class that also contains “Solutions producing osmotic diuresis,” which manage fluid balance through osmotic mechanisms. The category “Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions” is an inverse subclass of both “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” and “IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients,” reflecting that blood‑related products and perfusion fluids share functional and formulation characteristics with general IV and irrigating solutions. This category is also inversely linked to “blood and related products,” underscoring its inclusion of products that substitute or

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions comprise a broad therapeutic class that includes both irrigating solutions and intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, nutrients, and plasma‑derived or synthetic colloids. Within this hierarchy, the subcategory “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” contains other irrigating solutions (e.g., glycin) and salt irrigating solutions (e.g., sodium bicarbonate). The IV‑solution branch contains formulations that affect electrolyte balance; the carbohydrate/electrolyte group links agents that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective drugs. The “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions” subgroup includes O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl‑starch. Dextran is further divided into dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75, each representing distinct molecular‑weight fractions employed for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 158
retrieved_docs.content: Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions represent a broad therapeutic class that encompasses irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, perfusion agents, and intravenous solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, as well as blood and related products. Within the irrigating‑solutions subgroup, the categories “Other irrigating solutions in ATC” and “Salt irrigating solutions” are each classified under the group “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion.” “Other irrigating solutions in ATC” is associated with glycin, whereas “Salt irrigating solutions” is associated with hydrogenuhličitan sodný (sodium bicarbonate). Carbohydrate/Electrolytes belong to the class “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance,” which itself is a subclass of the intravenous solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. These carbohydrate/electrolyte preparations are linked to formulations that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents. Both “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance” and “Solutions producing osmotic diuresis” are types of intravenous solutions used for parenteral fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient delivery. This hierarchical organization clarifies how various fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient delivery systems—ranging from irrigating and perfusion solutions to specialized carbohydrate/electrolyte and osmotic‑diuretic formulations—are interrelated, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate products for irrigation, perfusion, electrolyte management, and blood‑product replacement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2059
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS are categorized as a specific type within the broader group of irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and perfusion, indicating that they belong to this larger class of medical fluids used for irrigation, substitution of blood components, and perfusion purposes.
